title.en: Cloud Foundations: Tenants, Subscriptions & Management Groups

description.en: Understand how Azure organizes everything you own — from the Entra tenant at the top, through management groups, down to subscriptions and resource groups — and why getting this hierarchy right on day one saves years of pain.

story_variants.en:
## The Skeleton of Every Azure Estate

Before you deploy a single virtual machine, Azure has already made you answer a question most teams skip: *where does this thing live, and who governs it?* The answer is a four-level hierarchy that every enterprise Azure estate is built on:

- **Entra tenant** — the identity boundary. One tenant = one directory of users, groups, and app registrations. Almost every company has exactly one production tenant (plus maybe a sandbox tenant for testing risky identity changes).
- **Management groups** — folders for subscriptions, up to **six levels deep** below the tenant root group. Policy and RBAC assigned here *inherit downward*.
- **Subscriptions** — the unit of billing, quota, and blast radius. A subscription is a hard boundary: limits like vCPU quotas, API throttling, and many service limits apply per subscription per region.
- **Resource groups** — lifecycle containers inside a subscription. Delete the group, delete everything in it.

> **Callout:** The tenant is an *identity* boundary; the subscription is a *resource and billing* boundary. Confusing the two is the single most common conceptual error new Azure architects make.

## Why Enterprises Run Dozens of Subscriptions

A startup can live in one subscription. An enterprise cannot, for three concrete reasons:

1. **Quota isolation.** vCPU quota, storage account counts (250 per subscription per region by default), and ARM API request throttling are all per-subscription. One noisy team can starve another.
2. **Blast radius.** A compromised service principal with Contributor at subscription scope can only wreck *that* subscription.
3. **Clean chargeback.** Cost Management rolls up naturally when 'one subscription = one workload/environment' — no tag archaeology required.

The pattern Microsoft's Cloud Adoption Framework recommends is *subscription democratization*: treat subscriptions like cattle. Vend a new one per landing zone (workload + environment) from a template, rather than nursing one giant shared subscription.

## Management Groups: Governance Without Copy-Paste

Management groups exist so you can say things **once**. A typical enterprise tree looks like this:

| Level | Group | What gets assigned here |
|---|---|---|
| 0 | Tenant Root Group | Almost nothing — break-glass RBAC only |
| 1 | `contoso` (intermediate root) | Global deny policies (allowed regions, blocked services) |
| 2 | `platform` | Policies for identity, connectivity, management subscriptions |
| 2 | `landing-zones` | Workload guardrails: require tags, deny public IPs, enforce diagnostics |
| 2 | `sandbox` | Looser policies, budget caps, auto-expiry conventions |
| 2 | `decommissioned` | Deny-all policy for retiring subscriptions |

An Azure Policy assigned at `landing-zones` automatically applies to every subscription that ever gets placed there — including ones created next year by a team you have never met. That is the whole point: **governance by placement, not by ticket**.

> **War story:** A retailer once assigned a 'deny public IP' policy directly on 40 individual subscriptions instead of one management group. Six months later, subscription 41 arrived without the policy, a contractor exposed a database VM to the internet, and the incident review's first finding was: *the guardrail existed 40 times and still missed*. Inheritance is not a convenience feature; it is a correctness feature.

## Rules of Inheritance You Must Memorize

- RBAC and Policy assigned at a management group flow down to **every** child group, subscription, resource group, and resource.
- A child can *add* assignments but can never *remove* an inherited deny. Policy exemptions exist, but they are explicit, auditable artifacts.
- A subscription lives under exactly **one** management group at a time, but can be *moved* — and when it moves, it instantly picks up the new parent's policies and drops the old ones. Moving a subscription is therefore a governance event, not an org-chart tidy-up.
- New subscriptions land in the **default management group** (configurable; set it to `sandbox` or an `onboarding` group, never the root).

## Naming and Design Guidance

- Keep the tree **shallow**: 3–4 levels is plenty. Every level adds cognitive and policy-evaluation overhead. Do not mirror your org chart — org charts reorganize yearly; workload archetypes do not.
- Split by **archetype** (platform vs. corp vs. online vs. sandbox), not by department.
- Reserve the tenant root group: assign a break-glass Owner group there and lock everything else down. Many orgs also enable Microsoft Entra **elevated access** for one global admin only during emergencies.
- Name subscriptions predictably: `sub-<workload>-<env>-<seq>` (e.g., `sub-payments-prod-001`). Your FinOps team will thank you.

## Common Pitfalls

- **Everything in one subscription** 'to keep it simple' — until quota exhaustion, tangled RBAC, and a 4,000-line cost export make it anything but.
- **Deep trees** that mirror the org chart, then a reorg forces mass subscription moves and policy churn.
- **Assigning at root** because it is convenient. A bad deny policy at root can block deployments tenant-wide, including the platform team trying to fix it.
- **Ignoring the default management group**, so unmanaged 'shadow' subscriptions accumulate with zero guardrails.
- **Treating dev/test/prod as resource groups** inside one subscription instead of separate subscriptions — you lose quota isolation and cheap environment-scoped RBAC.

## Mental Model to Carry Forward

Think of the hierarchy as a **policy funnel**: decisions get more specific as you descend. Broad, rarely-changing rules (allowed regions, security baselines) live high; workload-specific settings live low. When you design it this way, onboarding a new application team becomes: create subscription → place under `landing-zones` → done. Governance is already there waiting for them — which is exactly what the rest of this track will build on.

description.en: The four-level Azure hierarchy: identity at the top, resources at the bottom, governance flowing downward through management groups.

left.label: One giant shared subscription for everything

right.label: Subscription per workload+environment under management groups

description.en: Subscriptions are quota, billing, and blast-radius boundaries — vend them like cattle, don't nurse one pet.

animation_sequence.code:
az account management-group create --name landing-zones
az account management-group subscription add \
  --name landing-zones \
  --subscription sub-payments-prod-001

explanation.en: Create a management group — a folder for subscriptions that policy and RBAC inherit through.
Move a subscription under it. The subscription instantly inherits every policy assigned at landing-zones.

description.en: Expanding a management group shows child subscriptions and the policies they inherit without any per-subscription assignment.

stages: Team deploys freely inside the rails

description.en: Governance by placement: the subscription vending flow that scales to hundreds of teams.

instruction.en: Enterprises resolve governance by walking the management-group tree. Build a hierarchy path resolver: given HIERARCHY (a dict mapping each management group to its children) and SUBSCRIPTIONS (the list of subscription names), implement find_path(target) that returns the list of node names from 'root' down to the target. Then, for each subscription in SUBSCRIPTIONS order, print exactly: '<sub>: <path joined by " > ">' — e.g. 'sub-erp: root > workloads > corp > sub-erp'. This mirrors how Azure computes inherited policy scope for every subscription.

practice_challenges.starter_code:
HIERARCHY = {
    'root': ['platform', 'workloads'],
    'platform': ['sub-identity', 'sub-connectivity'],
    'workloads': ['corp', 'online'],
    'corp': ['sub-erp'],
    'online': ['sub-web'],
}
SUBSCRIPTIONS = ['sub-identity', 'sub-connectivity', 'sub-erp', 'sub-web']

def find_path(target, node='root'):
    # TODO: return list of names from 'root' to target (inclusive),
    # or None if target is not under node. Recurse through HIERARCHY.
    pass

# TODO: for each subscription, print '<sub>: <path joined by " > ">'
practice_challenges.expected_output:
sub-identity: root > platform > sub-identity
sub-connectivity: root > platform > sub-connectivity
sub-erp: root > workloads > corp > sub-erp
sub-web: root > workloads > online > sub-web

en: Base case: if node == target, return [node]. Otherwise recurse into each child in HIERARCHY.get(node, []).
When a recursive call returns a non-None path, prepend the current node: return [node] + child_path.
Join with ' > ' (space, greater-than, space) and print in the exact SUBSCRIPTIONS order.

practice_challenges.test_code:
assert find_path('sub-erp') == ['root', 'workloads', 'corp', 'sub-erp']
assert find_path('sub-identity') == ['root', 'platform', 'sub-identity']
assert find_path('sub-web') == ['root', 'workloads', 'online', 'sub-web']
assert find_path('nonexistent') is None
assert find_path('root') == ['root']

q.en: Which statement about the Entra tenant is correct?

en: It is the billing boundary for all resources
It is the identity boundary containing users, groups, and app registrations
It is a container that holds resource groups directly
Each subscription requires its own tenant

q.en: How many levels of management groups can you nest below the tenant root group?
A policy assigned at the 'landing-zones' management group is inherited by...

en: Only subscriptions that existed when it was assigned
Every current and future subscription placed under that group
Only resource groups tagged with the group name
Nothing — policies must be assigned per subscription

q.en: Why do enterprises prefer many subscriptions over one shared subscription?

en: Subscriptions are free while resource groups cost money
Azure limits each subscription to 10 resource groups
Quotas, throttling limits, and blast radius are per-subscription boundaries
Management groups only accept one subscription each
